---
title: "Ch 13 — Statistics (Variance and Standard Deviation)"
subtitle: "How spread out is the data? Mean deviation, variance, and standard deviation"
lesson-id: "cbse11.statistics"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 13
ncert-chapter-title: "Statistics"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse10.statistics"]
unlocks: ["cbse12.probability"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "databases", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 40
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you compute the **mean** of a small data set?
2. Are you fluent with the **median** and **mode** from Class 10
   statistics?
3. Can you compute $\sum x_i$ and $\sum x_i^2$ for a small list?
4. Do you know the symbol $\bar{x}$ (read "x-bar") for the mean?

Three confident yeses — read on. If shaky, refresh
**[Class 10 Statistics](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/13-statistics/index.qmd)**.
:::

## Touch

Two friends each scored an average of $80$ on five tests:

- Asha: $78, 80, 80, 82, 80$
- Bina: $60, 70, 80, 90, 100$

Same mean. But Asha is *consistent*; Bina swings wildly. The mean alone
doesn't tell you how far the data are from the mean.

We need a number for **spread**. The widget below computes mean and
standard deviation for a tunable data set.

```{ojs}
viewof n1 = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 78, step: 1, label: "data point 1"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof n2 = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 80, step: 1, label: "data point 2"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof n3 = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 80, step: 1, label: "data point 3"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof n4 = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 82, step: 1, label: "data point 4"})
```
```{ojs}
viewof n5 = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 80, step: 1, label: "data point 5"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const xs = [n1, n2, n3, n4, n5];
  const m = xs.reduce((s, x) => s + x, 0) / xs.length;
  const variance = xs.reduce((s, x) => s + (x - m) ** 2, 0) / xs.length;
  const sd = Math.sqrt(variance);
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>Data:</b> [${xs.join(", ")}]</div>
    <div><b>Mean</b> x̄ = <b>${m.toFixed(2)}</b></div>
    <div><b>Variance</b> σ² = (1/n) Σ(xᵢ − x̄)² = <b>${variance.toFixed(2)}</b></div>
    <div><b>Standard deviation</b> σ = <b>${sd.toFixed(3)}</b></div>
  </div>`;
}
```

The standard deviation is the typical distance from the mean. Make all
data points equal: $\sigma = 0$ (no spread). Spread them out: $\sigma$
grows.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. Why do we square the deviations $(x_i - \bar{x})$ before averaging?
   What if we just summed them?
2. The mean deviation uses $|x_i - \bar{x}|$ (absolute value) instead of
   the square. Why does the standard deviation feel more natural in
   physics and machine learning?
3. If you add $5$ to every data point, how do the mean and standard
   deviation change?
4. If you multiply every data point by $3$, how do they change?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) is the reason for squaring: $\sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$ always.
The deviations cancel. (3) and (4) are the **shift and scale** rules:
adding shifts the mean, scaling stretches both mean and SD.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Mean

Find the mean of $4, 8, 6, 5, 12$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\bar{x} = (4 + 8 + 6 + 5 + 12)/5 = 35/5 = 7$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Mean deviation about the mean

For the data above, find the mean deviation about the mean.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\text{MD} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum |x_i - \bar{x}|$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|4 - 7| + |8 - 7| + |6 - 7| + |5 - 7| + |12 - 7| = 3 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 5 = 12$.

$\text{MD} = 12/5 = 2.4$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Variance

For the same data, find the variance.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x_i - 7)^2$: $9, 1, 1, 4, 25$. Sum $= 40$.

$\sigma^2 = 40/5 = 8$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Standard deviation

For the same data.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sigma = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Quick formula

Use the alternative formula $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2$
on the same data.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sum x_i^2 = 16 + 64 + 36 + 25 + 144 = 285$.

$\sigma^2 = 285/5 - 49 = 57 - 49 = 8$. ✓
</details>

### Problem 6 — Effect of shift and scale

If $\bar{x} = 50$ and $\sigma = 5$, find the new mean and SD if every
data point is replaced by $3 x_i + 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Mean: $a x + b$ has mean $a \bar{x} + b$. SD scales by $|a|$, the shift
$b$ is irrelevant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
New mean: $3 \cdot 50 + 2 = 152$.

New SD: $|3| \cdot 5 = 15$.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Frequency distribution

Find mean and variance of:

| $x$ | 4 | 8 | 11 | 17 | 20 | 24 |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| $f$ | 3 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 4 | 3 |

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$. $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum f_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{\sum f_i}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sum f = 29$.

$\sum f x = 12 + 40 + 99 + 85 + 80 + 72 = 388$. $\bar{x} = 388/29 \approx 13.38$.

$\sum f x^2 = 48 + 320 + 1089 + 1445 + 1600 + 1728 = 6230$.

$\sigma^2 = 6230/29 - 13.38^2 \approx 214.83 - 179.02 \approx 35.8$.

$\sigma \approx 5.98$.
</details>

You have just used **mean**, **mean deviation**, **variance**,
**standard deviation**, the **shortcut formula**, **shift-and-scale**
rules, and **frequency-weighted** versions. Now we name them.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

For data $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ with mean $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum x_i$:

**Mean deviation** about $\bar{x}$:
$$ \text{MD} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - \bar{x}|. $$

**Variance:**
$$ \sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2. $$

**Shortcut formula:**
$$ \sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2 = \dfrac{1}{n^2} \left[ n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2 \right]. $$

**Standard deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$.

**Frequency-weighted version:** for $(x_i, f_i)$ with $N = \sum f_i$:
$$ \bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{N}, \quad \sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum f_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i^2}{N} - \bar{x}^2. $$

**Shift-and-scale:** if $y_i = a x_i + b$, then $\bar{y} = a \bar{x} + b$
and $\sigma_y = |a| \sigma_x$.

**Coefficient of variation:** $\text{CV} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ %.

:::

A short verse:

> *Square each deviation, then average — that is variance;*
> *Square-root that, the spread is no fluke.*
> *Shifts won't move it, scaling stretches by $|a|$;*
> *Compare two data sets — coefficient of variation rules.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the mean and variance of the first $n$ natural numbers.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1 + 2 + \cdots + n}{n} = \dfrac{n(n+1)/2}{n} = \dfrac{n + 1}{2}$.
2. $\sum x_i^2 = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}$.
3. $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} - \left(\dfrac{n+1}{2}\right)^2$
   $= \dfrac{(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} - \dfrac{(n+1)^2}{4}$
   $= (n+1) \left[\dfrac{2(2n+1) - 3(n+1)}{12}\right] = (n+1) \cdot \dfrac{n - 1}{12} = \dfrac{n^2 - 1}{12}$.

**Answers:** $\bar{x} = (n+1)/2$, $\sigma^2 = (n^2 - 1)/12$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Find the mean of $2, 4, 6, 8, 10$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$30/5 = 6$.
</details>

**W2.** Variance of the constant data $5, 5, 5, 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
All deviations are $0$. $\sigma^2 = 0$.
</details>

**W3.** SD of $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the shortcut formula or the result for first $n$ naturals.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\bar{x} = 3$. $\sigma^2 = (5^2 - 1)/12 = 24/12 = 2$. $\sigma = \sqrt{2}$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Find the mean and SD of $6, 7, 10, 12, 13, 4, 8, 12$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$n = 8$. Compute $\sum x$ and $\sum x^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sum x = 6 + 7 + 10 + 12 + 13 + 4 + 8 + 12 = 72$. $\bar{x} = 9$.

$\sum x^2 = 36 + 49 + 100 + 144 + 169 + 16 + 64 + 144 = 722$.

$\sigma^2 = 722/8 - 81 = 90.25 - 81 = 9.25$.

$\sigma = \sqrt{9.25} \approx 3.04$.
</details>

**P2.** The mean and SD of $20$ observations are $10$ and $2$
respectively. Each observation is multiplied by $3$. Find the new mean
and SD.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
New mean: $3 \cdot 10 = 30$. New SD: $3 \cdot 2 = 6$.
</details>

**P3.** Compute the variance of the frequency distribution:

| $x$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| $f$ | 1 | 4 | 6 | 4 | 1 |

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$N = 16$. $\sum f x = 2 + 16 + 36 + 32 + 10 = 96$. $\bar{x} = 96/16 = 6$.

$\sum f x^2 = 4 + 64 + 216 + 256 + 100 = 640$.

$\sigma^2 = 640/16 - 36 = 40 - 36 = 4$. $\sigma = 2$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** The mean of $5$ observations is $4.4$ and their variance is
$8.24$. If three of the observations are $1, 2, 6$, find the other two.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let the missing values be $a, b$. Two equations: sum and sum of squares.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1 + 2 + 6 + a + b = 5 \times 4.4 = 22 \Rightarrow a + b = 13$.

$\sigma^2 + \bar{x}^2 = \dfrac{\sum x^2}{n}$, so $\sum x^2 = 5(8.24 + 19.36) = 5 \cdot 27.6 = 138$.

$1 + 4 + 36 + a^2 + b^2 = 138 \Rightarrow a^2 + b^2 = 97$.

$(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 \Rightarrow 169 = 97 + 2ab \Rightarrow ab = 36$.

So $a, b$ are roots of $t^2 - 13 t + 36 = 0$, giving $t = 4$ or $t = 9$.

**Answers:** $4$ and $9$.
</details>

**S2.** The variances of two data sets are equal but their means differ.
The first has $20$ observations with mean $10$; the second has $30$
observations with mean $20$. Find the variance of the **combined** data
set if each variance is $4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Combined variance =
$\dfrac{n_1 \sigma_1^2 + n_2 \sigma_2^2 + n_1 (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x})^2 + n_2(\bar{x}_2 - \bar{x})^2}{n_1 + n_2}$
where $\bar{x}$ is the combined mean.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Combined mean: $\bar{x} = \dfrac{20 \cdot 10 + 30 \cdot 20}{50} = \dfrac{800}{50} = 16$.

Variance numerator: $20 \cdot 4 + 30 \cdot 4 + 20 (10 - 16)^2 + 30 (20 - 16)^2
= 80 + 120 + 20 \cdot 36 + 30 \cdot 16 = 200 + 720 + 480 = 1400$.

Combined variance: $1400/50 = 28$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** Variance of the data $4, 4, 4, 4$ is
(a) $0$ (b) $1$ (c) $4$ (d) $16$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
All equal — no spread. **Answer: (a).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the standard deviation of $3, 6, 9, 12, 15$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\bar{x} = 9$. Deviations: $-6, -3, 0, 3, 6$. Squares: $36, 9, 0, 9, 36$.
Sum $= 90$. $\sigma^2 = 18$. $\sigma = \sqrt{18} = 3\sqrt{2}$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Find the variance of the frequency
distribution:

| $x$ | 6 | 10 | 14 | 18 | 24 | 28 |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| $f$ | 2 | 4 | 7 | 12 | 8 | 4 |

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute $\bar{x}$, then variance via the shortcut formula.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$N = 37$. $\sum f x = 12 + 40 + 98 + 216 + 192 + 112 = 670$. $\bar{x} = 670/37 \approx 18.108$.

$\sum f x^2 = 72 + 400 + 1372 + 3888 + 4608 + 3136 = 13476$.

$\sigma^2 = 13476/37 - (670/37)^2 = 364.21 - 327.91 = 36.30$.

$\sigma \approx 6.02$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** The mean and SD of $100$ observations are
$50$ and $5$. By an error, an observation $50$ was replaced by $100$.
Find the corrected mean and SD.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Adjust $\sum x$ and $\sum x^2$ by removing the bad value and adding the
correct one.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Original $\sum x = 100 \cdot 50 = 5000$.

If a $50$ was *added by mistake* and the true value was meant to be
something else — but per the problem, the recorded value $100$ should
be replaced by the correct $50$.

Corrected $\sum x = 5000 - 100 + 50 = 4950$. New mean: $49.5$.

Original $\sum x^2 = n(\sigma^2 + \bar{x}^2) = 100(25 + 2500) = 252500$.

Corrected $\sum x^2 = 252500 - 100^2 + 50^2 = 252500 - 10000 + 2500 = 245000$.

New variance: $245000/100 - 49.5^2 = 2450 - 2450.25 = -0.25$.

Negative — error. Recompute carefully:

$\bar{x}^2 = 2450.25$. New variance: $2450 - 2450.25 = -0.25$. Impossible.

Most likely the problem means: an observation was $100$ but was wrongly
recorded as $50$. Then we *add* $100$ and *remove* $50$:

Corrected $\sum x = 5000 + 100 - 50 = 5050$. New mean: $50.5$.

Corrected $\sum x^2 = 252500 + 10000 - 2500 = 260000$. New variance:
$2600 - 2550.25 = 49.75$. New SD: $\sqrt{49.75} \approx 7.05$.

(Boards problems often hinge on the direction of the correction —
read carefully.)
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 13 LA "missing observations
given mean and variance" matches Stretch S1.
**JEE pointer:** combined-variance formula (Stretch S2) is JEE Main
standard.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Standard deviation = mean deviation."** They differ. SD uses
   squared deviations (penalises outliers more); MD uses absolute
   values.
2. **"Adding a constant to every data point changes the SD."** No.
   The shift cancels in $(x_i - \bar{x})$. Only scaling changes the SD.
3. **"$\sigma^2 = \bar{x^2} - \bar{x}^2$" — sometimes written
   incorrectly.** The correct shortcut is $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2$,
   i.e. $\overline{x^2} - (\bar{x})^2$.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you compute mean, variance, and SD on a small data set
   without the shortcut formula?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you state how mean and SD respond to shift and scale?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Probability](../14-probability/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S1 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** drill the variance shortcut on five fresh data sets.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Class 10 — Statistics](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/13-statistics/index.qmd)** —
  mean, median, mode for grouped data.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 12 — Probability](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/13a-conditional-probability-bayes/index.qmd)** —
  expectation and variance of random variables generalise this.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 13 — Statistics** (NCERT 2023 rationalised).
  Sections 13.1–13.5: mean deviation, variance, standard deviation for
  ungrouped and frequency distributions.
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t1.data-and-average`](../../tier-1-foundations/data-and-average/index.qmd) and
  [`t5.variance-clt`](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/variance-clt/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Standard deviation is the natural unit of "uncertainty".
Bayesian models, confidence intervals, and Gaussian noise assumptions
all measure spread in $\sigma$.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases / Analytics.** Outlier detection, anomaly flagging, and
quality control charts use $\bar{x} \pm 3\sigma$ as default
thresholds.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / Science.** Standard deviation is the unit in which
experimental error is reported. "Three sigma" detection in particle
physics and "two sigma" significance in social science studies are
literal multiples of $\sigma$.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: NRICH (Asha vs Bina problem framing), Russian school
(Discover sequence forces both definitions and the shortcut formula),
Bhāskara (verse on the squaring trick).
:::
